html:
Today, a massive Russian air assault on Kyiv marks a severe escalation in the conflict, dominating global attention as Ukrainian counter-strikes continue to disrupt Russia's oil sector and trigger fuel shortages. Meanwhile, delicate US-Iran discussions proceed amidst significant regional and internal Iranian developments following the death of Ayatollah Seyyed Ali Khamenei.

Russia-Ukraine Conflict: Kyiv's mayor has declared a day of mourning after a "most massive" Russian drone and missile attack killed at least 21 people and injured over 80. Damage was recorded in 30 locations, largely residential buildings, prompting Dublin Volodymyr Zelenskyy to press for an Irish government investigation into alumina exports to Russia. This major assault comes as Ukraine's extended campaign against Russian energy infrastructure continues to disrupt fuel supplies for Moscow, leading Kazakhstan to agree to supply gasoline to Russia. Amidst these developments, Vladimir Putin signals intent to further entrench his position.

US-Iran Relations and Middle East Security: The US and Iran held separate but reportedly positive discussions in Qatar with Qatari and Pakistani mediators, agreeing to continue talks. These "technical talks" paused for Ayatollah Seyyed Ali Khamenei’s funeral, which his successor, Masoud Pezeshkian, described as the "beginning of a new chapter" for Iran, even as ultra-hardliners accuse Ghalibaf and Pezeshkian of a ‘coup’ over the US deal and an Iran parliament deputy warns of US revenge for Khamenei killing. Amidst these dynamics, Iran warned oil tankers to use approved routes in the Strait of Hormuz or face a ‘forceful response’, while blaming the US for a ship grounding; the Strait has since reopened, potentially leading to an oil surplus. Separately, an explosion in a Damascus cafe killed at least five and wounded 16.

US Politics and Global Economy: US president Donald Trump has drawn criticism for allegedly hijacking the US’s 250th anniversary to serve his own agenda, according to a House Democrat report. He also faces scrutiny for refusing to renew the USMCA trade agreement with Canada and Mexico, and for his cryptocurrency ventures, which reportedly earned him over $1 billion last year. OpenAI is reportedly in early-stage talks to give the US government a 5% stake in the company to smooth relations with Donald Trump’s administration. Globally, the latest Guardian Essential poll finds Australians now view Israel more negatively than China, and perceptions of Donald Trump have soured since his re-election.

China's Global Reach: China's new ethnic unity law, which demands minorities integrate or face consequences and reportedly targets people overseas, has drawn concern from the EU and a bipartisan group of US lawmakers. Concurrently, China is pulling ahead in global research rankings and expanding its ambitions in space, positioning itself as a rival to the United States in technological leadership.

Humanitarian and Security Crises: Papua separatists claim to have shot dead a US pilot who transported Indonesian troops, torching his plane in what they called a ‘message’ to the US and Indonesia. In Venezuela, rescue efforts continue after twin earthquakes, with a survivor pulled alive after eight days, though humanitarian needs remain pressing. This follows the US deporting individuals to Venezuela just hours before the quakes struck. The Gaza war has now marked 1,000 days since October 7, 2023, with 90% of the strip reportedly ‘destroyed’ and 80% ‘seized’ by Israel.

Other Major World Events: The Vatican has declared the traditionalist Society of St. Pius X in schism, excommunicating its bishops and priests in what The New York Times describes as the first major crisis for Pope Leo. Meanwhile, Canada is celebrating its birthday, but Mark Carney is battling to keep the nation intact as independence movements in two provinces consider leaving the country. West African cocoa farmers are facing a financial blow after prices plummeted nearly 75%.
